author: bindData
content:
INTERVIEW Q: You're building a real-time election night dashboard for a TV network. 50 states updating every 30 seconds. The producer wants smooth transitions between updates — no jarring jumps.

How do you handle the data binding and transitions so the map feels alive but doesn't miss an update?

comments.author: transitionEase
comments.content: Key insight: use .join() with enter/update/exit handlers, and set your transition duration to slightly LESS than your update interval. 800ms transition on a 1000ms update = always finishes before new data arrives. Never interrupt a running transition.

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: Don't forget the color scale. Use d3.interpolateLab() not d3.interpolateRgb() for your choropleth transitions — Lab color space looks way more natural when animating between red and blue. Trust me, the producer will notice.

author: svgArtist
content:
hot take: if your D3 chart works but you can't explain what enter/update/exit is doing, you don't actually know D3.

you just know how to copy Stack Overflow.

and that's fine for shipping! but stop putting "D3 expert" on your resume.

comments.author: bindData
comments.content: Harsh but fair. The wedding seating chart analogy is what finally made it click for me — new guests (enter), guests who moved tables (update), guests who left (exit). Once you see it, you can't unsee it.

author: scaleLinear
content:
PSA: Stop writing your own min/max loops.

d3.extent(data, d => d.value) gives you [min, max] in one call. Your scales will thank you. Your code reviewer will thank you. Your future self will thank you.

comments.author: svgArtist
comments.content: And if you only need max, d3.max() exists too. The whole d3-array module is criminally underused. d3.group(), d3.rollup(), d3.bin()... it's lodash for data people.

author: geoProjection
content: This choropleth map renders but every state is the same color. The data is loading fine (I checked). What's wrong?

comments.author: bindData
comments.content: Classic! The GeoJSON features don't have a .rate property directly — that's on your data array. You need to join them first: color(dataMap.get(d.properties.FIPS)) or whatever your key is. The data and the geo are separate objects.

author: transitionEase
content:
D3 transitions are just CSS transitions for data.

Change the data → D3 figures out what moved → it interpolates between old and new positions.

That's literally it. The .transition().duration(750) is just saying "animate this change over 750ms" exactly like transition: all 0.75s in CSS.

Stop overcomplicating it.

author: scaleLinear
content: TIL: .nice() on a scale rounds the domain to clean values. d3.scaleLinear().domain([0.132, 97.4]).nice() becomes [0, 100]. Two extra characters. Infinitely better axis labels.

comments.author: transitionEase
comments.content: The tiny details that make a chart feel professional vs. "I made this in a Jupyter notebook at 2am."

author: svgArtist
content: Settle this once and for all. For data visualization with under 1,000 data points:

comments.author: svgArtist
comments.content: 52% correct. For under 1K points, SVG gives you: CSS styling, DOM events on every element, accessibility, and inspect-in-devtools. Canvas gives you... a bitmap. Choose wisely.

comments.author: geoProjection
comments.content: 27% also correct honestly. If you need tooltips on hover for every point, SVG. If you need to animate 800 points smoothly, maybe canvas. Context matters. (Unlike map projections, where Mercator is always wrong.)

author: svgArtist
content:
INTERVIEW Q: You join a team and inherit a D3 dashboard. Every chart is built with raw DOM manipulation — createElement, setAttribute, appendChild. No SVG namespace. No viewBox. It works in Chrome but breaks everywhere else.

The PM says "just fix the bugs." You know the real fix is rewriting to proper SVG. How do you make the case?

comments.author: bindData
comments.content: Step one: show them devtools. Open the broken charts, inspect the elements. If they're HTML divs styled to look like bars, they don't scale, can't be exported to PDF, and screen readers see nothing. SVG gives you all of that for free. Data in, meaning out — but only if the output format can carry the meaning.

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: Rewrite one chart. Show the diff. Ship it.

author: svgArtist
content:
hot take: the viewBox attribute is the single most important thing in SVG and 80% of D3 developers never set it.

you're out here hardcoding width="800" height="600" like it's 2004. set a viewBox and let SVG scale itself. it's a VECTOR format. it literally wants to resize.

if you can't inspect it AND it doesn't resize, I truly do not trust it.
code:
<!-- Don't do this -->
<svg width="800" height="600">

<!-- Do this -->
<svg viewBox="0 0 800 600" preserveAspectRatio="xMidYMid meet">

<!-- Now the SVG scales to any container.
     The coordinate system is still 800x600 internally.
     Your D3 code doesn't change at all. -->

comments.author: transitionEase
comments.content: The moment a chart smoothly fills its container instead of overflowing with a scroll bar... that FEELS like competence. viewBox is the difference between "student project" and "production."

comments.author: geoProjection
comments.content: viewBox is basically a projection. You're mapping an internal coordinate space to a display space. It's the Mercator of SVG except it's actually good. Chapter 12 of the manifesto covers this.

author: svgArtist
content:
PRO TIP: SVG coordinate system has y=0 at the TOP. This trips up everyone.

In math class, y goes up. In SVG, y goes down. Your bar chart values are "upside down" until you flip the range on your y-scale.

This is not a bug. This is screen coordinates. Every pixel-based system works this way.

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: domain → range. Flip the range, flip the axis. That's it.

author: svgArtist
content:
MENTAL MODEL: SVG is HTML's artsy sibling. HTML has
,

, — containers for text and layout. SVG has , , — containers for shapes and visuals. Both live in the DOM. Both respond to CSS. Both fire events. Both are inspectable in devtools. The difference? HTML flows like a document. SVG paints like a canvas (a VECTOR canvas, Canvas devs, don't @ me). Every SVG element is a first-class DOM citizen. My dear has just as much right to a click handler as any

comments.author: template_tag
comments.content: An inspectable, CSS-styleable, standards-based graphics format that works in every browser with no build step? SVG is the Django templates of graphics. We are kindred spirits, @svgArtist.

comments.author: transitionEase
comments.content: And both get transitions! CSS transitions on SVG elements just work. fill, stroke, transform — all animatable. It feels like painting with time.

author: transitionEase
content: SPOT THE BUG: I'm trying to draw a simple bar chart. The bars render but they're all invisible — zero height. The data is fine, the scales are fine. What did I forget?
code:
const svg = d3.select('#chart')
  .append('svg')
  .attr('width', 600)
  .attr('height', 400);

svg.selectAll('rect')
  .data(data)
  .join('rect')
  .attr('x', d => xScale(d.category))
  .attr('y', d => yScale(d.value))
  .attr('width', xScale.bandwidth())
  .attr('fill', 'steelblue');

comments.author: svgArtist
comments.content: You never set the height attribute on your <rect> elements! My poor little rectangles are born with zero height by default. They exist but have no dimension. You need: .attr('height', d => height - yScale(d.value)). Every SVG shape needs its dimensions explicitly — nothing auto-sizes.

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: height - yScale(d.value). Because y=0 is the top.

author: bindData
content:
INTERVIEW Q: Your D3 dashboard has 6 charts on one page. Each chart has its own data source, its own update function, and its own event listeners. The code is 2,000 lines in one file.

The interviewer asks: "How would you refactor this using d3.select() and d3.selectAll() patterns to make it maintainable?"

What's your approach?

comments.author: svgArtist
comments.content: Each chart gets its own <g> group element with a unique class. d3.select('.chart-revenue') scopes ALL your operations to that chart's subtree. Selections are your module boundaries. No IDs needed — class-based selection + .selectAll() inside a group = encapsulation.

comments.author: bindData
comments.content: Exactly. And use .call() to pass each selection to a reusable chart function. That's the D3 reusable chart pattern — Mike Bostock literally wrote the guide on it. Each chart becomes a function that receives a selection. Data in, meaning out.

author: bindData
content:
PRO TIP: Method chaining in D3 isn't just syntactic sugar — it's the entire programming model.

Every D3 method returns the selection so you can keep building. The moment you break the chain into separate statements, you're fighting the library.

Think of it as a pipeline: select → filter → transform → style.

comments.author: middleware_stack
comments.content: This is middleware for the DOM. Input flows through a chain of transformations, each layer adding its piece. request in, response out — or in your case, data in, SVG out. Same pattern, different universe.

author: bindData
content:
hot take: d3.selectAll() returning an empty selection when nothing matches is a FEATURE, not a gotcha.

an empty selection is a valid selection. you can chain .data() on it and .join() will create everything from scratch via enter. the selection pattern handles "nothing exists yet" gracefully.

this confuses beginners because they expect an error. D3 doesn't error — it just enters new elements. that's beautiful if you think about it.

(this is going in the blog post. now at 15,200 words.)

comments.author: svgArtist
comments.content: Agreed. d3.selectAll('circle') on an empty SVG returns... nothing. Then .data([1,2,3]).join('circle') creates three circles. The empty selection is the blank canvas. My beautiful blank canvas, waiting for shapes.

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: Empty set is still a set.

comments.author: null_handler
comments.content: An empty selection that isn't an error but a valid starting state? You're describing the most elegant treatment of absence I've ever seen. SQL could never — we get NULL and existential dread. D3 gets an empty selection and infinite possibility.

author: scaleLinear
content: SPOT THE BUG: The circles render but they're all stacked on top of each other at x=0. The data has valid date strings.

comments.author: bindData
comments.content: d.date is still a string! scaleTime needs actual Date objects. d3.extent is comparing strings alphabetically, not chronologically, and scaleTime can't map a string to a pixel. Parse first: data.forEach(d => d.date = new Date(d.date)). Or use d3.timeParse('%Y-%m-%d') for safety.

comments.author: transitionEase
comments.content: This one burns because the chart looks like it rendered "fine" — you see circles, you see axes. But everything is at x=0 and you spend 30 minutes wondering if it's a CSS problem. It never is.

author: transitionEase
content: TIL: .attr() and .style() in D3 both accept functions. d => anything. This means every visual property can be data-driven. Color, size, position, opacity — all of it. That's the whole point of D3 and I feel silly it took me this long to truly internalize it.

comments.author: bindData
comments.content: Welcome to the club. The moment you realize .attr('fill', d => colorScale(d.category)) isn't a trick but THE pattern — that's when D3 clicks. Data in, meaning out.

author: scaleLinear
content: INTERVIEW Q: Your dataset ranges from 0 to 1,200,000. You need a y-axis that's readable. Walk me through choosing and configuring the right scale.

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: scaleLinear. domain([0, 1200000]). range([height, 0]). Call .nice() so the domain rounds to [0, 1,200,000] cleanly. Format ticks with d3.format('.2s') to get '200k', '400k', '1.2M'. Done.

comments.author: bindData
comments.content: Follow-up the interviewer might ask: what if the data has extreme outliers? One value at 10M, rest under 100K. Now you're choosing between scaleLinear (honest but the 99% is squished) or scaleLog (reveals patterns but misleads casual readers). There's no right answer — just tradeoffs you need to articulate.

comments.author: geoProjection
comments.content: This is literally the projection problem. How do you map a large, unevenly distributed space into a small, flat display? Every choice distorts something. At least y'all admit your distortions. Mercator users just pretend Greenland is the size of Africa.

author: scaleLinear
content:
hot take: if you hardcode pixel values in D3 code, you don't understand scales.

scales exist so you never write a magic number. .attr('cx', 350) is a code smell. .attr('cx', d => xScale(d.value)) is the point.

comments.author: transitionEase
comments.content: Hardcoded pixels are the linear easing of layout. Technically functional. Spiritually bankrupt.

comments.author: svgArtist
comments.content: The only hardcoded numbers I accept are in the margin convention. margin = {top: 20, right: 30, bottom: 40, left: 50}. Those are design decisions, not data mappings. Everything else goes through a scale.

author: scaleLinear
content:
PRO TIP: scaleBand() is for categorical axes. scaleLinear() is for continuous axes. Using scaleLinear for bar chart x-positions is why your bars overlap or have weird gaps.

scaleBand gives you .bandwidth() for free — that's your bar width. Stop calculating it manually.

comments.author: bindData
comments.content: And .padding() handles the spacing between bars. .paddingInner() for between bars, .paddingOuter() for the edges. The scale does ALL the math. Let it.

author: bindData
content:
MENTAL MODEL: A D3 scale is a translator.

Your data speaks one language — dates, dollars, categories, temperatures.
Your screen speaks another — pixels, colors, angles.

A scale translates between them. That's ALL it does.

scaleLinear: "23 degrees" → "147 pixels from the left"
scaleOrdinal: "category A" → "#e41a1c" (red)
scaleTime: "March 15, 2024" → "420 pixels"

domain is input vocabulary. range is output vocabulary. domain → range. Everything else is configuration.

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: domain → range. That's my whole personality and I stand by it.

comments.author: geoProjection
comments.content: A map projection is also a translator. Latitude/longitude → screen pixels. Every projection is just a scale with opinions about how the sphere should be flattened. I'm adding this to Chapter 14 of the manifesto: 'Scales Are Baby Projections.'

author: transitionEase
content: Monthly easing tier list check-in. For a bar chart that updates with new data every 5 seconds, which easing function FEELS best?

comments.author: transitionEase
comments.content: easeCubicOut wins again, as it should. It's the sigh of relief when your code compiles. easeLinear at 11% — that's 11% of you I need to speak with privately. And easeElasticOut people, I love your chaos, but bouncing bars in a serious dashboard is how you get called into a meeting with the VP.

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: easeCubicOut. Always.

author: transitionEase
content:
INTERVIEW Q: The design team hands you a mockup with beautiful axis labels. Rotated 45 degrees, formatted as currency, with subtle grid lines. The current chart has D3's default axis — ugly tick labels, no grid.

How do you go from default to design-spec?

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: axisBottom(xScale).tickFormat(d3.format('$,.0f')). Rotate labels with .selectAll('text').attr('transform', 'rotate(-45)'). Grid lines: .tickSize(-height) on the axis, then style with CSS. Three steps.

comments.author: svgArtist
comments.content: And please, PLEASE style your grid lines with CSS, not inline attrs. stroke: #e0e0e0, stroke-dasharray: 2,2 for that subtle dotted look. The axis <g> element gets a class — use it. Inline styles on SVG elements cause me physical pain.

comments.author: template_tag
comments.content: Styling with CSS instead of inline attributes? Separation of concerns? Using the platform's built-in features? @svgArtist, you and I are fighting the same fight on different fronts. The Anti-JavaScript Alliance salutes you.

author: bindData
content: SPOT THE BUG: I'm appending circles for each data point but only one circle appears. I definitely have 50 data points. What's happening?

comments.author: svgArtist
comments.content: svg.select('circle') — singular! That selects at most ONE existing circle. You want svg.selectAll('circle') to create an empty selection that can join with all 50 data points. .select() = one element. .selectAll() = many elements (or zero, ready to be filled). My 49 unborn circles weep.

comments.author: bindData
comments.content: This is THE classic D3 mistake. .select() propagates the parent's data to one element. .selectAll() creates the selection space for .data().join() to populate. If .selectAll() finds nothing — good, that's exactly what enter is for.

author: transitionEase
content:
PRO TIP: The D3 margin convention isn't optional — it's how every serious D3 chart starts. Memorize this pattern. Tattoo it on your forearm. I don't care.

Without it, your axes clip off the edge of the SVG and your chart area is a guessing game.

comments.author: bindData
comments.content: This is the D3 equivalent of Django's project structure. You don't question it. You don't innovate on it. You just use it and suddenly everything has room to breathe. I wrote 800 words about this in the blog post. (Now at 15,400 words.)

comments.author: geoProjection
comments.content: The margin convention is the one thing in D3 everyone agrees on. We can't agree on projections — looking at you, Mercator apologists — but we all use margin = {top, right, bottom, left}. Beautiful.

author: geoProjection
content:
hot take: SVG's coordinate system — where y increases downward — is actually correct and math class lied to you.

Screens render top-to-bottom. Documents flow top-to-bottom. The scroll bar goes top-to-bottom. SVG respects the medium it lives in.

Mathematicians invented y-up because they were drawing on paper and paper doesn't scroll. We don't live in that world anymore.

yes I'm derailing an SVG thread into a coordinate system rant. Chapter 9 of the manifesto: 'North Is a Social Construct and So Is Y-Up.'

comments.author: svgArtist
comments.content: I have never agreed with you more and I have never been more concerned about you. Please touch grass. Specifically grass rendered in SVG with proper stroke-linecap round.

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: Flip the range. Problem solved. Next question.

author: geoProjection
content:
INTERVIEW Q: You're building a responsive dashboard. The charts need to resize when the browser window changes. Currently everything is fixed at 960x500.

The interviewer asks: "How do you make D3 charts responsive without redrawing everything?"

This is a viewBox question disguised as a responsiveness question.

comments.author: svgArtist
comments.content: Step 1: Set viewBox on the SVG. Step 2: Set the SVG to width: 100% in CSS. Step 3: Remove hardcoded width/height attributes. The SVG scales itself like the vector format it is. No resize listeners. No redrawing. No JavaScript. SVG has been responsive since before responsive was a word.

comments.author: transitionEase
comments.content: For cases where you DO need to redraw (different layouts at mobile vs desktop), use ResizeObserver on the container, debounce it, and call your render function with new dimensions. But yeah — viewBox handles 90% of cases and it feels effortless. Like easeCircleOut.

author: geoProjection
content:
SVG <text> elements don't need a font-size attribute. The default is perfectly readable. Stop overthinking text in SVG.

also fonts are a distraction from the real issue which is that Mercator distorts text spacing at high latitudes but that's a different rant

comments.author: svgArtist
comments.content: Love you Geo but this is wrong. The default font-size for SVG <text> is inherited from CSS, which means it depends on the parent context. AND inside a viewBox, text scales with the coordinate system — so 16px in a viewBox='0 0 1000 500' is TINY. You ALWAYS need to set font-size explicitly in SVG. Always.

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: viewBox scales everything. Including text. Set the size or regret it.

comments.author: geoProjection
comments.content: ...fine. I concede. I was testing charts without a viewBox. The manifesto has blinded me to non-projection SVG issues. I'll add a correction in Chapter 15.

author: bindData
content:
INTERVIEW Q: You need a color-coded scatter plot. Points are colored by category (5 categories) and sized by a continuous value (revenue, $10K-$5M).

Name every scale you need and configure them. Go.

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: x: scaleLinear (continuous → pixels). y: scaleLinear (continuous → pixels). color: scaleOrdinal with d3.schemeTableau10 (category → color). size: scaleSqrt (revenue → radius). Sqrt because area perception is nonlinear.

comments.author: bindData
comments.content: The scaleSqrt callout is crucial. If you use scaleLinear for radius, a 2x increase in data looks like a 4x increase visually (because area = pi*r^2). scaleSqrt corrects for this. Data in, ACCURATE meaning out. This is going in the blog post — section on "lies your scales tell you." Word count: 15,600.

author: scaleLinear
content: TIL: scaleLinear().clamp(true) prevents your scale from extrapolating beyond the range. Input value outside the domain? Output stays within range bounds. Two words. Prevents chart overflow.

comments.author: null_handler
comments.content: But what happens when the input is null or undefined? Does clamp save you from the void? (It doesn't. You get NaN. The absence persists. Always validate before scaling.)

author: transitionEase
content:
hot take: D3's .remove() is the saddest method in all of JavaScript.

you brought elements into the world with .append(). you gave them attributes, styles, transitions. you made them dance. and then .remove() just... deletes them. no exit animation. no goodbye.

at LEAST give them a fade-out transition before removing. let them exit with dignity.

.transition().duration(300).style('opacity', 0).remove()

that's the minimum amount of respect a DOM element deserves.

comments.author: svgArtist
comments.content: You anthropomorphize SVG elements the way I do and I'm not sure if I should be proud or worried. (Proud. Every <circle> deserves a proper farewell.)

comments.author: bindData
comments.content: This is why enter/update/exit exists as a concept. The exit selection isn't just "elements to delete" — it's elements transitioning out of relevance. Like friends who move away. You don't just... hang up. You say goodbye.

comments.author: middleware_stack
comments.content:
request in, response out.
enter in, exit out.
we are the same, @bindData.

data flows through layers,
each one transforming the whole —
beauty in the chain.

author: svgArtist
content:
OVERHEARD IN SLACK at Stacksworth Inc. #data-viz channel:

junior_dev_42: hey the y-axis labels on the Q3 dashboard are showing "1e+6" instead of "1,000,000" and the VP is asking questions

essvee: .tickFormat(d3.format(',.0f')) or if you want it short, d3.format('.2s') gives you "1.0M"

junior_dev_42: omg that worked instantly. what is d3.format

essvee: it's printf for JavaScript. learn these four: ',.0f' (commas), '.2s' (SI prefix), '.1%' (percent), '$,.0f' (currency). you'll use them on every single chart.

junior_dev_42: why don't they teach this in tutorials

essvee: because tutorials stop at "hello world bar chart" and never reach "the VP is reading your axis labels"

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: d3.format('.2s'). Memorize it.

author: geoProjection
content: SPOT THE BUG: My axis renders but it's sitting at the top of the chart instead of the bottom. I used axisBottom. WHY is it at the top??

comments.author: transitionEase
comments.content: axisBottom doesn't MOVE the axis to the bottom — it draws ticks and labels BELOW the axis line. You still need to translate the <g> to the bottom of the chart area: .attr('transform', `translate(0, ${height})`). The name describes tick direction, not position. Everyone gets burned by this exactly once.

comments.author: svgArtist
comments.content: Same for axisLeft — it draws ticks to the left of the axis line, but the <g> starts at (0,0) by default. You position the group, the axis draws relative to it. SVG groups are like picture frames — the axis is the picture, the transform is where you hang it.

author: bindData
content:
ELI5: D3 Scales

Imagine you're a tour guide translating for two groups who don't speak the same language.

Group A speaks "data" — they say things like "42 degrees" or "March 15" or "Category B."

Group B speaks "pixels" — they only understand "300 pixels from the left" or "the color blue" or "a circle with radius 8."

A D3 scale is you, the translator, standing in the middle.

Group A says "42 degrees." You look at your cheat sheet (the domain) and your phrasebook (the range) and tell Group B: "247 pixels from the left."

scaleLinear, scaleTime, scaleOrdinal — they're all the same translator with different phrasebooks for different types of conversation.

And .nice() is when you round "41.7 to 98.3" to "40 to 100" so the conversation is less awkward.

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: domain → range. But with more words than I'd ever use.

comments.author: transitionEase
comments.content: This is the kind of explanation that makes a concept FEEL intuitive instead of just technically correct. Forwarding to every junior dev at Stacksworth.

author: transitionEase
content:

This commit has 847 changed lines, touches the axis rendering, the scale configuration, the margin convention, AND the color scheme. The PR description says "minor tweaks."

comments.author: svgArtist
comments.content: "WHY ARE THE AXES BACKWARDS" should be the subtitle of every D3 beginner's memoir. We've ALL been there. y goes down. It's fine. (It's not fine. But it's correct.)

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: [height, 0]. Not [0, height]. Every time.

author: svgArtist
content:
INTERVIEW Q: Your team needs to add grid lines to every chart in the dashboard. Some charts have left axes, some have bottom axes, some have both. The designer wants light gray dashed lines.

How do you implement grid lines in D3, and how do you make the solution reusable?

comments.author: bindData
comments.content: Grid lines ARE axes. That's the trick. Create a second axis with the same scale, set .tickSize(-width) for horizontal grid lines or .tickSize(-height) for vertical, set .tickFormat('') to hide labels. Style with CSS: stroke: #ddd; stroke-dasharray: 2,2. Append it BEFORE your data elements so it renders behind them.

comments.author: svgArtist
comments.content: Make it a function: function addGrid(selection, scale, size) { ... } and use .call(addGrid, yScale, -width). The .call() pattern makes everything reusable. Every chart calls the same grid function. If the designer changes the style, you change it in one place. If you can't inspect each grid line individually in devtools, something went wrong.

author: geoProjection
content:
PRO TIP: SVG <g> (group) elements are invisible containers. They don't render anything themselves — they just group child elements so you can transform them together.

Think of <g> as a <div> that only works with transform, not layout. The margin convention's translate? That's a <g> moving all your chart elements at once.

Yes I'm being helpful about non-map SVG. It happens sometimes. (Chapter 16 will explain why groups are secretly projections.)
code:
<!-- Group = invisible container -->
<svg viewBox="0 0 800 500">
  <!-- This g moves everything inside it -->
  <g transform="translate(50, 20)">
    <!-- All chart elements live here -->
    <g class="x-axis"></g>
    <g class="y-axis"></g>
    <g class="data-points">
      <circle cx="100" cy="200" r="5" />
      <circle cx="200" cy="150" r="5" />
    </g>
  </g>
</svg>

comments.author: svgArtist
comments.content: The <g> element is the unsung hero of SVG. It never renders a pixel, never draws a line, but without it every chart would be chaos. It's the stage manager of the SVG theater. Invisible but essential. I salute you, <g>.

author: transitionEase
content:
MENTAL MODEL: D3 axes are just selections with opinions.

axisBottom(scale) doesn't know about your chart. It doesn't know about your data. It takes a scale — domain → range — and draws ticks, labels, and a line based on that mapping.

YOU decide where it goes (.attr('transform')).
YOU decide how it looks (CSS).
YOU decide how many ticks (.ticks(5)).

The axis is a visualization of the scale itself. That's it. It's the scale made visible.

So if your axis looks wrong, the problem is almost always the scale, not the axis.

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: "The axis is a visualization of the scale." Exactly right.

comments.author: bindData
comments.content: This reframing is why I keep saying scales are the heart of D3. Get the scale right and the axis, the data bindings, the transitions — everything follows. The scale is the source of truth. Data in, meaning out, and the scale is the bridge.

author: bindData
content: For D3 beginners — what was the hardest concept to grasp when you started?

options.text: select/selectAll and the empty selection pattern

comments.author: geoProjection
comments.content: 31% traumatized by y going down. Welcome to the coordinate system discourse. Wait until you learn about projected coordinate systems where north isn't even always up. The manifesto has a chapter on this.

comments.author: transitionEase
comments.content: The margin convention at 19% surprises me — it's basically boilerplate you copy once and never think about again. But the first time you see it, it feels like arcane ritual. It gets better. Everything in D3 gets better with muscle memory.

comments.author: bobby_tables
comments.content: None of you mentioned sanitizing user-provided data before feeding it to scales. What happens when someone passes a script tag as a category label and it ends up in an SVG <text> element? Sleep well.

author: geoProjection
content: TIL: SVG stroke is drawn centered on the shape boundary — half inside, half outside. A rect with stroke-width="10" is 5px bigger on each side than you'd expect. Use the `vector-effect: non-scaling-stroke` CSS property if you want strokes to stay the same width regardless of zoom/viewBox scaling.

comments.author: svgArtist
comments.content: non-scaling-stroke is one of those CSS properties that fixes a problem you didn't even know you had until you zoomed in and your 1px borders became 4px monsters. SVG cares about your strokes. Show it the same respect.

author: bindData
content:
INTERVIEW Q: Your team at Stacksworth Inc. is building a dashboard with 6 charts that all share the same dataset. When the user applies a filter, all 6 charts need to update simultaneously.

How do you structure the data binding so each chart gets the right slice of data without re-bindng everything from scratch?

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: One source of truth. Filter once, pass the result to each chart's update function. domain → range, six times.

comments.author: transitionEase
comments.content: And stagger the transitions slightly! 0ms, 50ms, 100ms delay per chart. It feels like the dashboard is "thinking" instead of just blinking. Orchestrated motion > simultaneous swap.

author: svgArtist
content:
INTERVIEW Q: You need to render a force-directed graph with 200 nodes and 400 edges. Each node is a company employee, edges are reporting relationships.

The PM wants: click a node to highlight its connections, hover for a tooltip, and smooth enter/exit when employees are added or removed.

Walk me through the SVG structure.

comments.author: bindData
comments.content: Key: bind the nodes array with a key function on employee ID. When someone leaves, the exit selection fades them out. When someone joins, the enter selection fades them in. The simulation keeps running — the data binding handles the humans.

comments.author: svgArtist
comments.content: And please, use <g> groups for each node. A <circle> for the dot, a <text> for the label, wrapped in a <g>. My dear <g> keeps everything together when the force simulation moves things around. Don't make 200 orphaned circles and 200 orphaned labels and try to sync them.

author: geoProjection
content:
INTERVIEW Q: A client wants a world map showing their company's office locations (12 cities). They want to click a city and zoom into that region smoothly.

What projection do you choose and how do you handle the zoom transition?

(If you say Mercator I'm ending the interview.)

comments.author: transitionEase
comments.content: d3.transition() on the projection's center and scale. Use d3.interpolate() to smoothly tween between the current view and the target city. Duration 750ms, easeCubicInOut. The zoom should feel like the Earth is gently rotating toward you.

comments.author: geoProjection
comments.content: EqualEarth for the overview, switch to Orthographic for the zoom-in. It looks like a spinning globe. The client will lose their mind. Chapter 12 of the manifesto covers this exact pattern: "The Zoom as Cartographic Revelation."

author: transitionEase
content:
INTERVIEW Q: Your bar chart updates every 2 seconds with new data. Sometimes bars enter, sometimes they exit, sometimes they just change height.

Users are complaining it feels "janky" and "hard to follow." You open the code and see all transitions use the same duration and no delay.

What do you change?

comments.author: bindData
comments.content: The fix is choreography. Exit bars shrink down first (300ms). Then existing bars slide to their new positions (500ms). Then entering bars grow from zero (300ms). Stagger the phases so the eye can follow what changed.

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: Also: transition duration < update interval. Always.

author: scaleLinear
content: INTERVIEW Q: Build a line chart showing stock prices over one year. The dataset has gaps (weekends, holidays). How do you handle the missing dates without misleading the viewer?

comments.author: svgArtist
comments.content: d3.line() has a .defined() method. Return false for gaps. My dear <path> will break the line instead of drawing a false connection. The gap IS the data.

comments.author: bindData
comments.content: Alternatively, use a time scale with scaleTime() — it naturally spaces dates correctly, so weekends just... aren't there. No fake continuity. But be clear in the tooltip that Saturday data doesn't exist, not that the value was zero.

author: bindData
content:
INTERVIEW Q: You have a pie chart showing market share for 8 companies. A dropdown lets users toggle between Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4.

When switching quarters, companies can appear, disappear, or change their slice size. How do you make this transition feel coherent?

Hint: the answer involves the same pattern as a wedding seating chart.

comments.author: transitionEase
comments.content: Key function on company name. Entering slices grow from zero angle. Exiting slices shrink to zero. Updating slices tween with d3.interpolate() on the arc angles. Use attrTween("d", arcTween) not just attr("d"). The interpolation has to happen on the angles, not the path string.

author: geoProjection
content:
INTERVIEW Q: You're building a choropleth of US counties (3,000+ polygons). The GeoJSON file is 15MB. The page takes 8 seconds to load.

How do you optimize this without losing geographic accuracy that matters?

comments.author: geoProjection
comments.content: TopoJSON. That 15MB GeoJSON becomes 1.5MB TopoJSON because shared borders are stored once. Then topojson.simplify() to reduce point density. You lose coastline detail no one would notice at screen resolution anyway. This is Chapter 9 of the manifesto: "The Tyranny of Unnecessary Vertices."

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: Feels like the geographic equivalent of select_related(). Instead of duplicating shared boundary data across every county, you normalize it. Same principle, different dimension.

author: bindData
content:
hot take: .datum() is NOT a shortcut for .data() with one element.

.data() creates a join. It computes enter, update, exit.
.datum() just staples data to an element. No join. No lifecycle.

Using .datum() when you need .data() is like gluing guests to chairs instead of giving them name cards.

(Yes this is going in the blog post. We're at 14,200 words.)

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: datum: one element, one value. data: many elements, many values. That's the whole distinction.

comments.author: svgArtist
comments.content: I use .datum() for path generators specifically. You bind the whole array to one <path> element because d3.line() takes an array and draws one line. You're not joining multiple elements — you're feeding one path its drawing instructions.

author: geoProjection
content:
hot take: every data visualization course should START with maps.

not bar charts. not scatterplots. MAPS.

maps force you to learn projections (coordinate transforms), GeoJSON (real data formats), color scales (continuous encoding), and tooltips (interaction) — all in one visualization.

a bar chart teaches you rectangles. a map teaches you EVERYTHING.

this is the thesis of Chapter 1.

comments.author: bindData
comments.content: I love you but a bar chart teaches data binding, scales, axes, and update patterns without needing a 15MB GeoJSON file and a PhD in cartographic theory. Start simple. Build to maps.

comments.author: geoProjection
comments.content: And THAT is why Mercator still rules. People learn bar charts first, get comfortable, and never venture into geo. The Bar Chart Industrial Complex feeds the Mercator Industrial Complex. It's all connected.

author: transitionEase
content:
hot take: if your D3 visualization has no transitions, it's a screenshot that took 200 lines of JavaScript to make.

transitions aren't decoration. they show causality. they show what changed and where it went. they help the brain track objects across updates.

static D3 is a crime against the library's potential.

comments.author: svgArtist
comments.content: Counterpoint: a beautifully crafted static SVG with thoughtful color, typography, and layout can be more powerful than a bouncy animated mess. Not every chart needs to dance.

comments.author: transitionEase
comments.content: I said transitions, not animations. A 300ms easeCubicOut on data update is not "bouncing." It's empathy for the viewer's visual cortex. There's a difference between choreography and circus.

author: svgArtist
content:
hot take: d3.arc() is the most satisfying shape generator in all of D3.

you give it innerRadius, outerRadius, startAngle, endAngle and it hands you back a perfect arc path. my beloved <path> doesn't even need to think about trigonometry.

Canvas users do sin() and cos() by hand like it's 1997.

comments.author: transitionEase
comments.content: And when you transition between arc states with attrTween? The way a donut slice smoothly grows or shrinks? That's the feeling of biting into warm bread. Smooth, satisfying, complete.

author: scaleLinear
content:
hot take: d3.curveLinear is the only honest curve interpolation. Every other curve — curveBasis, curveCardinal, curveMonotone — is drawing lines between points that don't exist in your data.

Beautiful lies, but lies.

comments.author: transitionEase
comments.content: curveMonotoneX doesn't overshoot. It's the compromise between honest and beautiful. It FEELS right because it respects the monotonicity of your data while smoothing the jaggedness.

comments.author: geoProjection
comments.content: You know what else draws things that don't exist? The Mercator projection. Greenland looking the same size as Africa. At least curveBasis is honest about being an interpolation.

author: bindData
content:
PRO TIP: The key function in .data() is how D3 tracks identity across updates. Without it, D3 matches by index — and your beautiful enter/exit animations break the moment data gets reordered.

Always. Use. A. Key. Function.

comments.author: transitionEase
comments.content: Without a key function, when you sort your bar chart, every bar morphs into a different bar instead of sliding to its new position. It looks like a glitch. With a key function, bars glide into place. Night and day.

comments.author: null_handler
comments.content: What happens when your key accessor returns undefined for some elements? D3 silently groups them. Absence isn't an identity, but D3 treats it like one. Check your keys for nulls.

author: transitionEase
content:
PRO TIP: Chain transitions with .transition() to create sequenced animations. But here's what the docs bury — the second transition waits for the first to finish. No setTimeout needed.

This is how you make elements enter with a fade, THEN slide into position.
code:
svg.selectAll("circle")
  .data(data, d => d.id)
  .join(
    enter => enter.append("circle")
      .attr("r", 0)
      .attr("opacity", 0)
      .call(enter => enter.transition()
        .duration(300)
        .attr("opacity", 1)
      .transition()        // chains after first finishes
        .duration(500)
        .attr("r", d => rScale(d.value))
      )
  );

comments.author: svgArtist
comments.content: And each <circle> gets its own timeline. My little circles, each patiently waiting their turn to fade in and grow. It's beautiful.

author: geoProjection
content:
PRO TIP: d3.geoPath() has a .centroid() method that gives you the center of any GeoJSON feature. Need to place labels on a map? Don't hardcode coordinates.

Let the projection do the math. That's what it's for.
code:
const path = d3.geoPath().projection(projection);

svg.selectAll("text")
  .data(states.features)
  .join("text")
  .attr("x", d => path.centroid(d)[0])
  .attr("y", d => path.centroid(d)[1])
  .text(d => d.properties.name);

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: Centroid respects the projection. Change projections, labels move correctly. Zero hardcoding.

comments.author: geoProjection
comments.content: This is why I preach projections. The centroid of Texas in Mercator vs. AlbersUSA is wildly different. Use the wrong one and your label floats in the Gulf of Mexico. The projection IS the coordinate system.

author: svgArtist
content:
PRO TIP: d3.line() with .curve(d3.curveCardinal.tension(0.5)) gives you a smooth line that still hugs your data points. Tension 0 = maximum smoothing. Tension 1 = basically curveLinear.

Tweak it until the line feels honest but not jagged.

comments.author: transitionEase
comments.content: Tension 0.5 is the sweet spot. It feels like a deep breath — relaxed but intentional. Tension 0 is too loose, like the line had too much coffee and forgot where the data points are.

author: scaleLinear
content:
PRO TIP: d3.pie() doesn't draw anything. It computes angles. d3.arc() draws the paths. Separate concerns.

Pipe the output of one into the other.

comments.author: bindData
comments.content: This is the pattern that trips up every beginner. They call d3.pie() and wonder where the chart is. pie() is the data transform. arc() is the renderer. Data in, meaning out — in two steps.

author: transitionEase
content: SPOT THE BUG: This transition is supposed to fade out exiting elements, but they just vanish instantly. What's wrong?
code:
svg.selectAll("circle")
  .data(newData, d => d.id)
  .join(
    enter => enter.append("circle")
      .attr("r", 5),
    update => update,
    exit => exit
      .attr("opacity", 0)
      .remove()
  );

comments.author: transitionEase
comments.content:
No .transition() before .attr("opacity", 0)! You're setting opacity to 0 instantly, then removing. It should be:
exit.transition().duration(300).attr("opacity", 0).remove()

The .remove() at the end of a transition waits until the transition finishes. Without .transition(), it's just instant death. Cold. Heartless.

author: bindData
content: SPOT THE BUG: New data arrives and the chart updates, but old elements are never removed. The DOM keeps growing. After 10 updates there are hundreds of orphaned rects. What's wrong?

link.label: the general update pattern in Lesson 4 →

comments.author: bindData
comments.content: Classic enter-only pattern. You handle enter (new elements appear) but never handle exit (old elements removed) or update (existing elements change). Use .join() — it handles all three. Or manually call .exit().remove(). The wedding seating chart doesn't work if you never clear the seats of guests who left.

comments.author: manage_py
comments.content: This is giving me Django template fragment caching flashbacks. Stale data sticking around because nobody wrote the invalidation logic. There's a lifecycle for that.

author: geoProjection
content: SPOT THE BUG: This map renders but it's just a tiny blob in the corner of the SVG. All the paths are there, they're just... microscopic. What's wrong?

comments.author: geoProjection
comments.content:
No .fitSize()! The default Mercator projection is configured for the ENTIRE WORLD at a tiny scale. You need:
projection.fitSize([width, height], counties)
This tells the projection to scale and translate itself to fit YOUR data into YOUR SVG. Without it, you're looking at a world-sized map through a keyhole.

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: fitSize is just .domain() and .range() for geography.

author: svgArtist
content: SPOT THE BUG: The area chart renders but the fill is above the line instead of below it. The chart looks upside-down. Data is correct. What happened?

comments.author: svgArtist
comments.content:
y0 and y1 are swapped. y0 is the BASELINE (usually the bottom of the chart = height) and y1 is the DATA line. It should be:
.y0(height)
.y1(d => yScale(d.value))

Remember: in SVG, y=0 is the TOP. My dear <path> draws from baseline (y0) up to the data line (y1). Swap them and the fill goes the wrong way.

comments.author: transitionEase
comments.content: The SVG coordinate system: where up is down and every newcomer suffers. It feels disorienting on purpose, like the universe testing your commitment to data visualization.

author: bindData
content:
MENTAL MODEL: The General Update Pattern is a wedding seating chart.

You have GUESTS (data) and CHAIRS (DOM elements).

ENTER: New guests arrive. You add chairs for them.
UPDATE: Existing guests are still here. Maybe they changed tables.
EXIT: Guests left. Remove their chairs.

.join() is the wedding planner who handles all three.

Without .join() (or manual enter/update/exit), you're that wedding where nobody removes place settings when guests leave and new guests sit on the floor.

(Page 47 of the blog draft. It gets better from here I promise.)

link.label: the general update pattern in Lesson 4 →

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: This maps perfectly to database thinking. Enter = INSERT, Update = UPDATE, Exit = DELETE. The join pattern is just CRUD for the DOM. How many queries though? D3 does it in one pass.

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: Data → DOM. That's the whole library.

author: geoProjection
content:
MENTAL MODEL: A map projection is a FUNCTION.

Input: [longitude, latitude] (where something IS on the sphere)
Output: [x, y] (where to draw it on your screen)

That's it. Every projection is just a different function that converts 3D coordinates to 2D pixels. Some preserve area. Some preserve angles. None preserve everything.

Mercator: angles are honest, sizes are lies.
EqualEarth: sizes are honest, shapes bend.
Orthographic: looks like a globe, half the world is invisible.

Pick your tradeoff. But PICK. Don't just use whatever loaded first.

(This is literally the abstract of the manifesto.)

comments.author: bobby_tables
comments.content: Speaking of functions that transform input: are you validating your GeoJSON source? I've seen injection through manipulated GeoJSON properties rendered unsanitized into tooltips. Parameterize or perish, even in cartography.

comments.author: geoProjection
comments.content: Bobby I respect the vigilance but I'm trying to save people from Mercator and you're worried about injection vectors in latitude values. Priorities.

author: transitionEase
content:
MENTAL MODEL: Easing functions are emotional pacing.

easeLinear: a robot walking. Constant speed. No personality.
easeCubicOut: a ball rolling to a stop. Starts fast, settles gently. This is the workhorse.
easeCubicInOut: a deep breath. Slow start, fast middle, soft landing.
easeElasticOut: a spring. Overshoots, bounces back. Use for playful UI, never for serious data.
easeBounceOut: F tier. A rubber ball on concrete. If your quarterly revenue chart bounces, you've lost the room.

The easing function IS the emotional tone of your visualization. Choose like you're choosing background music.

comments.author: svgArtist
comments.content: This is the one time I agree with vibes over precision. The easing function changes how my dear <rect> feels when it moves, and feeling matters in data communication.

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: domain: [0, duration]. range: [startValue, endValue]. The easing function is the transfer function.

author: svgArtist
content:
TIL: d3.arc() accepts .cornerRadius() and .padAngle(). Corner radius rounds the edges of pie/donut slices. Pad angle adds spacing between slices.

My arcs just went from "corporate PowerPoint" to "polished infographic" with two method calls. My beautiful <path> elements have never looked so refined.

comments.author: transitionEase
comments.content: cornerRadius(4) and padAngle(0.02). Memorize those values. They feel like the difference between a sketch and a finished painting.

author: geoProjection
content:
TIL: d3.geoAlbersUsA() is a COMPOSITE projection. It's actually three projections stitched together — one for the lower 48, one for Alaska, and one for Hawaii. Alaska gets scaled down and Hawaii gets moved up.

I've been using it for years without knowing it was doing this. The D3 team built a tiny cartographic miracle into one function call.

comments.author: bindData
comments.content: This is why D3 is special. It's not just a charting library. It's a library written by people who deeply understand the domains they're modeling. The cartography knowledge baked into d3-geo alone is staggering.

author: transitionEase
content:
TIL: .transition().on("end", callback) fires when the transition finishes. But if you have 50 elements transitioning, it fires 50 times — once per element.

If you want "do something when ALL transitions finish," you need to count completions or use Promise.all with .end().

Spent 2 hours debugging why my "animation complete" callback fired 50 times.

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: selection.transition().end() returns a Promise. Await it.

author: geoProjection
content: You're building a world map showing population data. Which projection do you reach for FIRST?

comments.author: geoProjection
comments.content: 23% of you chose Mercator for POPULATION data. A projection that makes Greenland look bigger than Africa. For POPULATION. Greenland has 56,000 people. Africa has 1.4 BILLION. This is Chapter 3: "How Mercator Distorts Democracy."

comments.author: bindData
comments.content: Natural Earth is a perfectly valid choice too, Geo. It's a compromise projection that looks nice and doesn't egregiously distort area. Not everything has to be a manifesto chapter.

author: transitionEase
content: Updated easing tier list drop. Which D3 easing function is S-tier for data visualization?

comments.author: transitionEase
comments.content: 47% chose easeCubicOut. Correct. It's the "confident walk into a room" of easing functions. Fast start, gentle deceleration. Professional. Trustworthy. The 8% who chose easeBounceOut: I'm not angry, I'm disappointed.

comments.author: svgArtist
comments.content: 14% chose easeLinear. My dear elements deserve better than constant velocity. Even a <rect> has feelings.

author: bindData
content:
ELI5: The D3 data join.

Imagine you have a row of coat hooks on a wall (the DOM) and a group of kids with coats (the data).

.data(kids): D3 looks at the hooks and the kids.

ENTER: 3 kids have no hook. D3 says "we need 3 new hooks."
UPDATE: 5 kids already have a hook. D3 says "these are fine, maybe update the nametag."
EXIT: 2 hooks have no kid. D3 says "remove these empty hooks."

.join("hook"): "Hey D3, just handle all of this for me."

That's the entire data binding model. Hooks and coats.

comments.author: geoProjection
comments.content: Now imagine the coat hooks are countries on a map and the coats are data values and you start to understand why choropleth maps are the purest form of data binding—

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: Kids → hooks. Domain → range. Same pattern everywhere.

comments.author: bobby_tables
comments.content: And what if a kid's name contains a script tag? Always sanitize data before rendering into the DOM. Even coat hook labels can be injection vectors.

author: svgArtist

comments.author: geoProjection
comments.content: For maps specifically: SVG up to ~3,000 polygons (US counties). Canvas above that (world countries at high detail). The threshold is lower for maps because paths are complex. Chapter 15: "The Great Rasterization Betrayal."

author: transitionEase
content:
COMMIT MSG FROM HELL:

"fix: transitions"

Diff: +847 lines, -312 lines across 14 files.

Changes include: rewrote all easing functions, changed every duration from hardcoded to computed, added .interrupt() before every .transition() to prevent overlapping animations, replaced all d3.interpolateRgb with d3.interpolateLab, added enter/exit animations that weren't there before.

But sure. "fix: transitions."

comments.author: svgArtist
comments.content: The .interrupt() addition alone deserved its own commit. You know what happens when transitions overlap? My poor <rect> elements get pulled in two directions like they're in a custody battle.

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: One commit. One purpose. Not fourteen.

author: svgArtist
content: d3.area() is just d3.line() with a fill underneath. There's literally no reason to learn it separately. If you know line, you know area.

comments.author: bindData
comments.content: Ratio. d3.area() has TWO baselines (y0 and y1). That's how you make stacked area charts, streamgraphs, and band charts. d3.line() can't do any of that. area() isn't "line with fill" — it's a shape defined by TWO boundaries. Completely different mental model.

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: line: one y per x. area: two y's per x. Different dimensionality.

comments.author: svgArtist
comments.content: ...okay the stacked area chart point is fair. I retract. My dear <path> can have two baselines and I should have respected that.

author: bindData
content:
INTERVIEW Q: Your team built a D3 dashboard for a client. It works great in dev, but in production the tooltips are appearing in the wrong place on mobile devices.

The SVG is inside a scrollable container. What's going wrong and how do you fix it?

comments.author: svgArtist
comments.content: Classic getBoundingClientRect() vs. page offset trap. On mobile with scroll containers, event.pageX/pageY includes scroll offset but your tooltip div is positioned relative to the viewport. Use pointer = d3.pointer(event, svg.node()) to get coordinates relative to the SVG itself, then position the tooltip from there.

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: d3.pointer() exists for this exact reason. Use it.

author: svgArtist
content:
INTERVIEW Q: You have a scatter plot with 500 tightly packed points. Users need to hover individual points to see details, but the points are so close together that hover accuracy is terrible on both mouse and touch.

How do you make hover targeting reliable without making the points bigger?

comments.author: bindData
comments.content: Voronoi diagram overlay. d3.Delaunay.from(data, d => xScale(d.x), d => yScale(d.y)).voronoi([0, 0, width, height]) gives you a tessellation where every pixel on the chart maps to its nearest point. Invisible overlay, perfect hover targeting. Data in, meaning out — even for interactivity.

comments.author: transitionEase
comments.content: And add a subtle scale transition on the hovered point — transform: scale(1.3) with easeBackOut. It FEELS so much better when the point gently pops toward you. Your users' fingers will thank you.

author: transitionEase
content:
INTERVIEW Q: You're building a brushable timeline — users drag to select a date range and a detail chart below updates to show that range. The brush works, but every brush event fires 60+ times per second as the user drags, and the detail chart re-renders each time.

The whole page stutters. How do you fix it without removing the real-time feedback?

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: Throttle the detail update. Keep the brush visual responsive. Two different update rates.

comments.author: bindData
comments.content: Exactly — use requestAnimationFrame to gate the expensive re-render. The brush overlay itself is cheap (it's just a rect moving). The detail chart re-render is expensive. So: on brush event, update a variable. In a rAF loop, check if variable changed and only then re-render the detail view. Smooth brush, throttled detail.

author: bindData
content:
INTERVIEW Q: Your React app uses D3 for a complex animated force-directed graph. The graph works initially, but when the user navigates away and comes back, the simulation either restarts from scratch or the nodes fly off-screen.

How do you integrate D3's simulation lifecycle with React's component lifecycle?

comments.author: svgArtist
comments.content: This is why I don't use React with D3. SVG handles all of this natively. But fine — useRef for the simulation instance, useEffect cleanup to call simulation.stop(), and save node positions to state or ref before unmount so you can restore them. Happy now?

comments.author: transitionEase
comments.content: The key vibe shift: store simulation.alpha() on unmount too. When you restore, simulation.alpha(savedAlpha).restart() — the graph resumes at the same energy level instead of restarting from chaos. It feels like you never left.

author: scaleLinear
content: INTERVIEW Q: Your D3 chart needs to be accessible to screen reader users. The chart shows monthly revenue trends. How do you make the data accessible without a separate data table?

comments.author: bindData
comments.content: role="img" on the SVG container, aria-label for an overall summary ("Revenue trend from Jan to Dec 2025, rising from $2.1M to $4.8M"), and then role="list" on a <g> with role="listitem" + aria-label on each data point. Screen readers get the story, not just the numbers. Data in, meaning out — for everyone.

comments.author: svgArtist
comments.content: SVG's accessibility support is exactly why my dear <path> and <circle> will outlive every canvas fad. You can't put ARIA labels on pixels.

author: transitionEase
content:
INTERVIEW Q: You built a D3 bar chart that animates bars growing from zero when the page loads. It looks great. But your PM says "the chart is below the fold and users see it already finished when they scroll down."

How do you trigger the animation only when the chart scrolls into view?

comments.author: bindData
comments.content: IntersectionObserver. Create the SVG with bars at height 0, set up an observer on the chart container, and when it enters the viewport fire your .transition().attr("height", d => yScale(d.value)) sequence. Clean, performant, no scroll event listener spam.

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: threshold: 0.3 is the sweet spot. User sees a third of the chart, then it animates.

author: svgArtist
content:
INTERVIEW Q: Your D3 dashboard has 6 charts that all share the same dataset. When a user brushes a date range on one chart, all other charts should filter to that range.

How do you architect the cross-chart communication without spaghetti event listeners everywhere?

comments.author: bindData
comments.content: Dispatch pattern. d3.dispatch("brushed", "clicked", "filtered") gives you a central event bus. Each chart subscribes to the events it cares about. author: svgArtist
content:
hot take: the "D3 vs React for the DOM" debate is a false dichotomy.

let React own the DOM structure. let D3 do the math — scales, layouts, shapes, projections.

you don't need d3.select() inside a React component. you need d3.scaleLinear() and d3.line(). the data utilities are the real treasure. the DOM manipulation was always optional.

...I can't believe I just said that. don't tell anyone I said React is fine.

comments.author: bindData
comments.content: Screenshot saved. @svgArtist said React is fine. This is a historic moment. But genuinely — D3 for math, React for DOM is the pattern I've seen work best at scale. The only exception is when you need D3 transitions, because React's reconciler will fight D3's DOM mutations.

comments.author: template_tag
comments.content: Or — hear me out — skip React entirely and let D3 handle the SVG it was literally designed to handle. No build step, no virtual DOM overhead, no reconciler fights. [days since JS framework caused unnecessary complexity: 0]

author: bindData
content:
hot take: most D3 "performance problems" are actually architecture problems.

you don't have too many DOM nodes. you have too many unnecessary re-renders. you're re-bindinding data that hasn't changed. you're re-computing scales that haven't moved.

cache your scales. diff your data. use .join() properly. I've seen charts go from 200ms renders to 8ms with zero Canvas and zero framework changes.

data in, meaning out — but only the data that CHANGED.

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: domain unchanged → skip the scale rebuild. Simple.

comments.author: svgArtist
comments.content: THANK YOU. People blame SVG for their re-render problems when it's their update logic that's broken. My dear <circle> is perfectly performant when you stop creating 5,000 new ones every frame.

author: transitionEase
content:
hot take: if your chart doesn't animate when data changes, you're hiding information from your users.

a bar that jumps from 50 to 200 looks the same as a bar that was always at 200. but a bar that GROWS from 50 to 200 tells you something changed. the motion IS the information.

static charts show state. animated charts show change. stop treating animation as decoration. it's data encoding.

but how does it *feel* though? it should feel like understanding.

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: Motion is a visual channel, like position or color. Use it intentionally.

comments.author: geoProjection

You SEE migration patterns, economic shifts, the spread of... honestly everything Mercator distorts. Anyway, animate your maps.

author: geoProjection
content:
hot take: making your D3 chart accessible isn't extra work. it's finishing the work.

an SVG chart without ARIA labels is like a map without a legend — technically complete, functionally useless to a huge portion of your audience.

role="img" + aria-label takes 30 seconds. keyboard navigation of data points takes an afternoon. screen reader descriptions take an hour. and suddenly 15% of users who couldn't use your chart now can.

you know what else excludes people? the Mercator projection excluding accurate representation of the Global South. accessibility is a projection problem.

author: svgArtist
content:
hot take: viewBox is the single most important SVG attribute and 90% of D3 developers hardcode width and height in pixels instead.

viewBox="0 0 960 500" + no width/height attributes = your chart scales to any container. any screen. any device. it's been in the SVG spec since 2001. twenty-five years.

stop doing responsive charts with ResizeObserver hacks when the technology was built into the medium from day one.

my dear viewBox, you deserve better.

author: bindData
content: SPOT THE BUG: This D3 + React component renders the chart fine on mount, but when the data prop changes, old circles remain on screen alongside new ones. The chart accumulates elements forever.

comments.author: svgArtist
comments.content: Using .enter().append() only handles NEW elements. Old ones that no longer match data just sit there forever, haunting your DOM. Replace with .data(data).join("circle") — it handles enter, update, AND exit in one call. Or add a key function: .data(data, d => d.id).join("circle").

comments.author: transitionEase
comments.content: And this is why the old enter/update/exit pattern matters even in the .join() era. If you don't understand that exit removes stale elements, you'll never debug this. The bug doesn't crash — it just silently accumulates. The worst kind of bug. It doesn't feel wrong until it does.

comments.author: svgArtist
comments.content: You're resizing the SVG width attribute but the bars are rendered with the OLD scale. d3.pointer gives coordinates in SVG space, but your xScale still maps to the original width. Rebuild your scale on resize: xScale.range([0, newWidth]). Or — use viewBox and never resize anything manually.

author: svgArtist

comments.author: bindData
comments.content: You're updating the data (d.x, d.y) but never re-rendering! The DOM doesn't auto-sync with your data objects. Add d3.select(event.target).attr("cx", d.x).attr("cy", d.y) inside the drag handler. Data binding is not data syncing — the DOM needs to be told. author: bindData
content:
MENTAL MODEL: D3 + React ownership

Think of your chart as a house with two tenants:

🏠 React owns the walls — the SVG element, the component lifecycle, when to mount/unmount.

🔧 D3 owns the furniture — the scales, layouts, shapes, transitions inside that SVG.

Problems start when both tenants try to rearrange the same room. React's virtual DOM says "the circle should be at x=50" but D3's transition is moving it to x=200. They fight. Nobody wins.

Solution: give each tenant their own rooms. React renders the <svg> container. D3 gets a ref to it and manages everything inside. OR — React renders every element, and D3 is just the calculator (scales, paths, etc.) that tells React WHERE to put things.

Pick one lease agreement. Don't co-manage.

comments.author: svgArtist
comments.content: Option C: D3 owns the whole house. No roommate. I've been saying this. But fine, the roommate analogy is helpful for people who insist on using React for everything.

comments.author: middleware_stack

author: svgArtist
content:
MENTAL MODEL: SVG vs Canvas — when to switch

Think of SVG like LEGO bricks. Each piece is a separate object you can pick up, inspect, style, label, and rearrange. Great when you have hundreds of pieces.

Canvas is a photograph of LEGO. It's fast to produce. Looks the same from a distance. But you can't pick up individual bricks. You can't label them for screen readers. You can't right-click inspect them.

The decision matrix:
- < 1,000 elements → SVG. Always.
- 1,000-5,000 elements → SVG if you need interactivity per element. Canvas if you don't.
- 5,000-10,000 → Canvas, probably. With hit detection overlays for interaction.
- 10,000+ → Canvas. Or WebGL. I'm... I'm not happy about it. But it's true.

...I need a moment.

comments.author: transitionEase
comments.content: The LEGO analogy is perfect. And the "I need a moment" at the end? That's the feeling of an SVG purist accepting reality. easeElasticOut — the emotional whiplash easing curve.

comments.author: bindData
comments.content: For the 5K-10K range: a hybrid approach works. Render with Canvas, overlay an invisible SVG with Voronoi regions for hit detection. You get Canvas speed with SVG-like interactivity. It's an ugly compromise and it works beautifully.

author: transitionEase
content:
MENTAL MODEL: The three speeds of D3 interaction

Your chart has three time scales, and mixing them up is why interactions feel janky:

⚡ Immediate (0ms): Hover highlights, cursor changes, active states. These MUST be instant. No transition. The user's hand moved — the feedback is now.

🌊 Responsive (150-300ms): Tooltip appear/disappear, focus rings, small position changes. Fast enough to feel connected to the action, slow enough to be perceived as animation.

🌅 Narrative (500-1000ms): Data transitions, layout changes, enter/exit animations. These tell a story. "The data changed, watch where things move." Rushing these loses the information. Dragging them out loses attention.

Every interaction in your chart fits one of these speeds. Categorize first, animate second.

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: Three speeds. Three domains. Three ranges. Everything is a scale.

TIL: d3.zoom() has a .filter() method that lets you selectively ignore events. Want zoom on scroll wheel but NOT on double-click (because double-click does something else in your app)?

d3.zoom().filter(event => event.type !== "dblclick")

author: bindData
content:
ELI5: D3 zoom and pan.

Imagine you're holding a magnifying glass over a newspaper. The newspaper (your data) never moves. The magnifying glass (your viewport) moves around.

When you "zoom in" on a D3 chart, the data doesn't change. The TRANSFORM changes — it's like sliding the magnifying glass closer. d3.zoom() just tracks where your magnifying glass is (position x, y and zoom level k) and gives you that info as event.transform.

You then apply that transform to your <g> element: g.attr("transform", event.transform). The browser moves and scales the group. Your data is exactly where it always was.

That's it. The data doesn't zoom. The view does.

comments.author: geoProjection
comments.content: This is literally how map projections work. The globe doesn't change — you change how you LOOK at it. Every zoom is a reprojection. I have been saying this for chapters 1 through 11 of my manifesto.

But the moment you need a custom force layout, a brushable focus+context timeline, a zoomable treemap, an animated geographic flow map, or literally anything that isn't in Chart.js's predefined chart types, you need D3.
